reasoning: Both models failed to review any files. The PKGBUILD was incorrectly classified as a 'binary file' and skipped by the file-type detection heuristic, resulting in zero meaningful analysis. No actual security assessment was performed by either model, so no verdict can be reached. This is a tooling failure, not a genuine safety finding.
disagreement_analysis: There is no substantive disagreement — both models hit the identical failure mode: the PKGBUILD was misdetected as a binary file and skipped, causing the audit to abort before any review occurred.
coverage_issues: PKGBUILD (the most critical file) was skipped entirely due to a binary-file misdetection bug
No .install hooks, source files, or supplementary scripts were reviewed because the audit aborted at the initial PKGBUILD check
false_positives: The 'binary file' classification of PKGBUILD is a false positive of the file-type detector — PKGBUILDs are plain text shell scripts and should never be treated as binary
false_negatives: Unknown — because no files were reviewed, any real risks in the PKGBUILD (download sources, build/package functions) remain entirely unassessed
re_audit_focus: PKGBUILD — force text-mode parsing and review download sources, build(), package(), and any prepare/check functions
Any .install scripts and source array URLs/checksums for the JDK documentation archive
learnings: The binary-file detection heuristic is incorrectly flagging PKGBUILD as binary; PKGBUILD should be whitelisted/forced to text mode since it is always a shell script
When the initial PKGBUILD audit cannot proceed, the tool should retry with a different parser or fail loudly rather than silently skipping and producing a 0-file 'skipped' result
A skipped-with-0-files outcome should be treated as a tool error requiring re-run, not as a near-verdict — both models trivially mirrored the same harness failure with no independent analysis
Consider detecting unusual encodings (e.g., non-UTF-8 or BOM/null-byte content) that trigger the binary classifier, and sanitize/decode before the file-type check
